data.title: Bitfarms winds down bitcoin mining, looking to more profitable HPC/AI
data.description:
Bitfarms (BITF) is winding down its bitcoin mining operations, in favor of focusing on high-performance compute/artificial intelligence infrastructure, it said on Thursday. The announcement when it said it's converting its Washington site to support HPC/AI workloads.

"We believe that the conversion of just our Washington site to GPU-as-a-Service could potentially  produce more net operating income than we have ever generated with bitcoin mining, providing the company with a strong cashflow foundation that could fund opex, G&A, and debt service and contribute to capex as we wind down our bitcoin mining business in 2026 and 2027," CEO Ben Gagnon said in a statement.

Bitfarms (BITF) stock dropped 11% in Thursday midmorning trading. The company also posted a bigger-than-expected loss in Q3 2025 amid the strategy shift.

data.title: xAI raises $15B in latest funding round to support GPU purchases: report
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk's artificial intelligence company, xAI (X.AI), has raised $15B in its latest funding round, according to a report by CNBC.

The funding round had garnered $10B by September, which propelled xAI to a valuation of $200B. It has since raised another $5B, elevating the current round to $15B. The report said much of the funds raised will be directed for the purchase of graphics processing units, or GPUs.    

xAI uses GPUs from both Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD). xAI's Colossus supercomputer in Memphis, Tenn., contains 200,000 GPUs, but xAI has said it has a roadmap for 1M GPUs.

xAI is the creator of the Grok large language model.

data.title: Kura Oncology wins FDA approval for leukemia drug
data.description:
• The U.S. Food and Drug Administration on Thursday approved ziftomenib (Komzifti), a leukemia drug developed by Kura Oncology (KURA) with Japanese drugmaker Kyowa Kirin (OTCPK:KYKOF) (OTCPK:KYKOY), triggering a trading halt in the California-based biotech.
  • In a press release, the FDA said it has approved the menin inhibitor for adults with relapsed or refractory acute myeloid leukemia ((AML)) with a nucleophosmin 1 (NPM1) genetic mutation for whom satisfactory alternative treatments are not available.
  • Previously, Kura’s (KURA) new drug application for ziftomenib was under the FDA’s priority review with a target action date of November 30.
  • “Bolstered by a strong balance sheet and our productive partnership with Kyowa Kirin, we are well-positioned to advance ziftomenib toward commercialization,” CEO Troy Wilson said with the company’s Q3 results last week.

data.title: OneWater Marine outlines $1.83B–$1.93B 2026 sales target as inventory and margin trends improve
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: OneWater Marine Inc. (ONEW) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Executive Chairman Philip Singleton reported, "We finished 2025 with solid results and meaningful progress on our strategic priorities. Industry conditions remain challenging as retail demand continued to normalize from pandemic highs, promotional activity increased and multiple hurricanes created disruption in key Florida markets." Singleton stated the company "delivered 6% same-store sales growth for the year, outperforming the broader industry in the categories where we compete." He highlighted that "new boat sales were strong in the fourth quarter, and pre-owned sales remained a standout throughout the year."
  • Singleton explained that the company completed its "strategic exit from discontinued brands, allowing us to sharpen our focus on our core portfolio with high-performing brands." He noted, "While this transition created some margin pain during the year, it laid the groundwork for meaningful long-term margin improvement as we move through 2026 and beyond."
  • CEO Anthony Aisquith added, "Despite a challenging marine market, our focus on serving customers drove another year of positive same-store sales growth and continued market share gains." Aisquith highlighted that "finance and insurance penetration remained healthy and continues to be a key strength. Further interest rate cuts should support customer affordability and enhance unit economics for boats financed through OneWater."
  • COO and CFO Jack Ezzell stated, "Fiscal fourth quarter 2025 revenue increased 22% to $460 million compared to $378 million in the prior year period, which was significantly affected by hurricane-related disruptions along the West Coast of Florida."

Outlook

  • Ezzell provided 2026 guidance: "We anticipate total sales to be in the range of $1.83 billion to $1.93 billion. We expect adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $65 million to $85 million and adjusted diluted earnings per share to be in the range of $0.25 to $0.75."
  • Management expects demand to fluctuate with traditional seasonal cycles and for "industry unit sales [to] be flat to this year." Ezzell acknowledged, "Our forecasted sales will be negatively impacted by the impact of brands we exited. However, we also expect to outperform a flat market. Accordingly, we expect these factors to offset, resulting in flat same-store sales for the year."
  • Management cited "improved industry inventory levels, reduced discounting and lower interest rates" as tailwinds for 2026, tempered by ongoing market uncertainty.

Financial Results

  • Ezzell reported, "New boat sales were up 27% to $275 million in the fourth quarter, while pre-owned sales increased 25% to $91 million. Overall, same-store sales were up 23%. Revenue from service parts and other sales for the quarter increased 7% to $81 million."
  • Gross profit increased to $104 million in the fourth quarter compared to $91 million in the prior year, driven by higher new boat volumes. SG&A expenses rose 6% to $84 million. SG&A as a percentage of sales was 18%, down 270 basis points.
  • Fourth quarter operating loss was $130 million, and adjusted EBITDA was $18 million. Net loss for the fiscal fourth quarter totaled $113 million or ($6.90) per diluted share, largely due to noncash goodwill and intangible asset impairments of $146 million.
  • Full year 2025 revenue increased 6% to $1.9 billion. Same-store sales increased 6%. Full year gross profit decreased 2% to $427 million. Gross profit margin for fiscal year 2025 was 23%. Net loss for fiscal year 2025 was $116 million or ($7.22) per diluted share. Adjusted EBITDA was $70 million and adjusted earnings per diluted share was $0.44.
  • Total liquidity was in excess of $67 million. Total inventory as of September 30, 2025, decreased to $540 million compared to $591 million in the prior year. Total long-term debt was $412 million, with net leverage of 5.1x trailing 12-month adjusted EBITDA.

Q&A

  • Craig Kennison, Baird: Asked about year-over-year inventory changes and the outlook for fiscal '26. Ezzell responded, "We're down roughly 8.5%, $50 million year-over-year... with the timing of some model year '26 boats... we started that build a little earlier this year because some of our stores were actually getting a little light on inventory."
  • Kennison: Inquired about inventory assumptions for fiscal '26. Ezzell stated, "I would expect it to be up modestly just with price increases and some things like that."
  • Kennison: Asked about interest rate expense outlook for 2026. Ezzell replied, "I think we have another 50 basis points of cuts in the model going this year... floor plan interest will be, let's call it, flattish to up slightly. And then our term interest would be -- should be down some, just as we continue to make amortization payments... down the 5% to 10% range."
  • Joseph Altobello, Raymond James: Asked about consumer rates and impact on demand. Singleton answered, "They've come down. I mean, they haven't dropped like a point, but they move with every rate cut, they start to move down."
  • Altobello: Queried about Fort Lauderdale Boat Show sales. Singleton confirmed, "We were almost up 20% for the show just slightly under that compared to last year, which is really good. But... we started to see that margin pressure go away, which is exciting."
  • Altobello: Asked about the promo environment for fiscal '26. Singleton said, "I think the promotional environment is going to stay put until the manufacturers start feeling the industry dealers... are ordering more boats."
  • Noah Zatzkin, KeyBanc: Asked about pre-owned dynamics and trade-ins. Singleton explained, "That momentum has kind of continued on... We are seeing more trades than we saw pre-COVID."
  • Zatzkin: Inquired about M&A activity. Singleton stated, "We're staying extremely disciplined on that. I mean, we're really focused on the debt right now... we'll probably be pretty disciplined and focused mainly on the debt."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on inventory management, interest rate impacts, and demand trends, with questions often seeking clarification on outlook and drivers for key metrics.
  • Management maintained a cautiously optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly expressing confidence while also noting uncertainty. Phrases such as "we are cautiously optimistic" and "momentum seems to be pretty decent right now" were common, with some hesitancy on interest rate forecasts.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from defensive regarding industry headwinds and tariffs to more constructive, highlighting strategic advances and improved inventory dynamics. Analysts' tone similarly shifted from concern over tariffs and margin compression to a greater focus on operational execution and future drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter guidance for 2026 projects total sales of $1.83 billion to $1.93 billion, with adjusted EBITDA of $65 million to $85 million and adjusted EPS of $0.25 to $0.75, compared to the previous quarter's full year guidance for 2025 of $1.8 billion to $1.85 billion in revenue, adjusted EBITDA of $65 million to $80 million, and adjusted EPS of $0.50 to $0.75.
  • Management completed its planned exit from discontinued brands this quarter, whereas last quarter the exit was in progress.
  • Margin pressure is noted as easing, with expectations for improvement in 2026, whereas last quarter, margin compression from promotions and brand exits was a key concern.
  • Analysts in the previous quarter focused on tariff effects and pre-owned sales mix, while this quarter's questions prioritized inventory, interest rates, and growth catalysts.
  • Management's confidence has improved, emphasizing execution, clean inventory, and a foundation for long-term value.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted macroeconomic uncertainty, promotional activity, and hurricane disruptions as key risks for 2025.
  • The company cited noncash goodwill and intangible asset impairments of $146 million, reflecting the decline in market capitalization relative to book value.
  • Management is focused on disciplined inventory management, cost controls, and reducing leverage as primary risk mitigation strategies.
  • Analysts raised concerns about future interest rate movements, inventory trends, and the sustainability of recent margin improvements.

Final Takeaway

OneWater Marine concluded the year with strong execution on strategic priorities, including a clean exit from selected brands, disciplined inventory management, and cost actions that position the company for stability and long-term margin improvement. The company set 2026 sales guidance of $1.83 billion to $1.93 billion and anticipates flat same-store sales, with adjusted EBITDA and earnings per diluted share targets reflecting cautious optimism in the face of ongoing market uncertainty. Management emphasized its competitive advantage in inventory positioning and its readiness to capture demand as industry conditions stabilize.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
